quote: But after I put that that you know, I after I changed, but they went up to about, you know, 200 and the first one came out to 209, and this one came out to 224.
stewart_read: Hiram has just disclosed that going all-electric tripled his bill — from $79 to $209-$224. This is not a complaint; it is a qualification signal. A customer whose bill tripled because they converted every appliance to electric is exactly the high-usage profile solar is built for. The rep's 'Oh, wow. Yeah. That's a huge jump' acknowledges the number but drops the emotional thread immediately. Strategic read: the rep may have been holding the flip for after bill capture. Reactive read: the rep had no flip ready and defaulted to logistics. The reactive read holds — the flip never came, not even after the bill was captured.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep was deliberately deferring the emotional anchor until after bill capture to avoid losing momentum on the technical task.
coaching_implication: Show the rep the codex flip pattern: the bill number Hiram gave ($79 → $224) is the leverage. A suggested rephrase (intent only, not verbatim): acknowledge the pain, then redirect it — 'That jump is exactly why we're talking. That's the number we're going to work on.' The manager should ask the rep: what did you do with that number after you heard it?

quote: Because it jumped from 60 to $2.40 because, you know, the last the last two months, I have the new the new system.
stewart_read: Second unprompted bill-pain disclosure. Hiram is connecting his new all-electric system to the bill spike — he understands the cause and is telling the rep. The rep responds 'Okay. Gotcha. That's good to know.' and pivots to the cost-vs-usage display problem. This is the 'capture-and-drop' failure the codex names explicitly. The rep has now missed the flip twice.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep was mid-navigation on a complex bill-retrieval task and couldn't split attention between emotional anchoring and technical guidance.
coaching_implication: The manager should use this moment to illustrate that bill-pain disclosures don't require a full pivot — a single sentence of alignment ('That's the number we're going to cut into') keeps the emotional thread alive without losing the navigation thread. The rep needs a short, repeatable bridge phrase she can deploy without breaking stride.

quote: for the 02/2025, my average was $66.39. Okay? I think it was that was monthly. Okay? So now from that, it jumped to $2.29 the last two, you know, the last last month.
stewart_read: Third unprompted bill-pain disclosure. Hiram is now providing historical context — he knows his pre-electric average ($66) and his post-electric bills ($229). He is doing the rep's emotional work for her. The rep responds 'Okay.' and immediately asks 'Are you guys seeing the usage I was talking about?' The pattern is now three-for-three: capture, acknowledge minimally, return to logistics.
coaching_implication: Three missed anchors on the same pain signal is a pattern, not a moment. The manager should play all three back-to-back in the 1-on-1 and ask: 'What was Hiram telling you each time? What did you do with it?' The goal is for the rep to hear the pattern herself.

quote: I got the program. I went electric. So I don't have I don't have no gas. What I got is electrical. Everything's electrical. I got the, the, the stove, the dryer. I got the system from the AC and the furnace together.
stewart_read: Hiram is describing a fully electrified home — stove, dryer, AC, and furnace all on electric. This is a high-load profile that makes him an above-average solar candidate. The rep does not acknowledge the load profile as a qualification signal; she stays focused on the bill-capture task. This is not a failure of procedure but a missed opportunity to build curiosity: a fully electric home with a tripled bill is a stronger solar candidate than a partial-electric home.
coaching_implication: The manager should flag this as a qualification signal the rep can use to build urgency: 'Your whole house runs on electricity — that's actually the profile where solar makes the biggest difference.' This doesn't require numbers; it requires the rep to recognize what the customer just told her.

quote: Oh, because, you know, he has some other company too. They came you know, they they got in contact with me too. Mhmm. So, you know yeah.
stewart_read: Hiram surfaces a competitor mid-scheduling. The rep's response — 'Okay. Yeah. We can make it super quick then since you've already been through the process once' — is a missed differentiation moment. The codex's already_saw_design rebuttal calls for comparison framing: 'We offer different equipment than most companies and our price points can be different.' The rep instead uses the competitor's presence as a reason to compress the appointment, which signals low confidence in Ion's value.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep may have been trying to reduce friction by framing the appointment as low-effort, not low-value.
coaching_implication: The manager should walk the rep through the already_saw_design rebuttal. The move is not to argue against the competitor but to position the Ion appointment as the comparison that makes the decision possible. 'You've already seen one design — ours will give you something to compare it against' is the frame.

quote: another company told me that I I'm, you know, the the panelists are free, and they don't have to pay anything, you know. And, you know, another company says, you know, a different story, you know.
stewart_read: Hiram raises a 'free panels' claim from a competitor. The rep does not address it, does not create curiosity about what Ion's design would show, and does not flag it as a question for the specialist. She validates the shopping behavior ('I would do the same thing if I were you') and moves to scheduling. This is not a scope-creep failure — the rep correctly avoids pricing the system. But she also misses the curiosity-creation move: the 'free panels' claim is a confusion signal that a well-placed question could have turned into appointment motivation.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep may have judged that engaging the free-panels claim would open a debate she couldn't win without the specialist's numbers.
coaching_implication: The manager should show the rep the curiosity-creation move: 'That's actually a great question for the specialist — there are a few different ways companies structure that, and he'll show you exactly how ours compares.' This keeps scope discipline while making the appointment feel like the answer to Hiram's confusion, not just a calendar item.

quote: K. And then I'll block off an hour for you, Hiram. So feel free to ask as many questions as you need to. Okay?
stewart_read: The button-up is incomplete. The rep confirms the time and blocks an hour, but misses: (1) phone-appointment specification — the transcript suggests an in-person visit ('stop by'), which is a different appointment type than the codex's phone-appointment protocol; (2) no 'is that fair' verbal handshake; (3) no computer/no-driving anchor; (4) no text-followup promise. The appointment is booked but without the structural anchors that reduce no-show risk. Given Hiram's explicit price-shopping posture, the missing handshake and confirmation elements make this appointment more fragile than it needs to be.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep may have judged that Hiram was cooperative enough that the full button-up protocol was unnecessary.
coaching_implication: The manager should walk through the button_up checklist against this call. The 'is that fair' handshake is the single highest-leverage missing element — it creates a verbal commitment from a customer who just said he won't commit. The rep should practice deploying it even when the customer seems cooperative.
